key_findings.finding: Hungarian matching was toxic to training
key_findings.evidence: Teacher forcing guarantees strict 1-to-1 alignment (slot i = GT mask i). Hungarian matching scrambled this perfect alignment, causing the model to fight the matching noise. Removing it immediately doubled val mIoU (0.30 → 0.59 at epoch 3).

key_findings.finding: Qwen bypass in Phase 1 is critical for clean vision engine training
key_findings.evidence: Feeding pre-computed GT embeddings directly to projector (bypassing Qwen forward) reduced VRAM from 75GB to 30GB and gave the projector+SAM a stable, noise-free training signal. Phase 1 val mIoU reached 0.657 vs 0.296 with live Qwen.

key_findings.finding: SAM3 LoRA capacity was a bottleneck
key_findings.evidence: Upgrading from r=8 targets=[q,v] to r=32 targets=[q,k,v,out_proj] (16x more parameters) enabled SAM to adapt its attention patterns for texture boundaries. This combined with the other fixes pushed val mIoU from 0.30 to 0.81.

key_findings.finding: LM loss dominates gradients when trained from epoch 1
key_findings.evidence: In run_1, LM loss consumed 94% of the gradient budget by epoch 20, starving segmentation. Val mIoU plateaued at 0.316. Reducing lm_weight from 0.5 to 0.1 helped but didn't solve the root cause.

key_findings.finding: CLIP embeddings are a lossy bottleneck
key_findings.evidence: CLIP projects to 512-dim foreign space requiring text_align_head (4096→512). Replacing with native Qwen 4096-dim self-distillation eliminated the information loss. Direct comparison in same embedding space is cleaner.

key_findings.finding: Phase 2 (Qwen LoRA unfreezing) improves val but hurts test generalization
key_findings.evidence: Phase 2 pushed val mIoU from 0.657 to 0.813 (+24%), but RWTD test mIoU dropped from 0.678 (epoch 5) to 0.544 (epoch 30) — a 20% decline. Qwen adapted to ADE20K scene distribution, losing texture crop generalization.

key_findings.finding: Inference gap: val mIoU >> test mIoU on SAME distribution
key_findings.evidence: At epoch 35, val mIoU=0.813 but ADE20k_Detecture test mIoU=0.547 (92% image overlap with training). The gap is entirely in the inference path — teacher forcing gives perfect embeddings, but inference_forward (autoregressive generation) produces weaker/wrong embeddings and incorrect k_pred.

On ADE20k_Detecture: 25% predict k=1 (under-segmentation), 20% predict k=6 (over-segmentation). Model has no count supervision during training (teacher forcing always provides correct count). Median k_pred=5 vs typical k_gt=3-4.

run_4_architecture_fix.key_change: no Hungarian, Qwen bypass, SAM r=32

critical_insight: The projector + SAM3 segmentation path works well when given clean embeddings (Phase 1 val mIoU=0.657, Phase 2 val mIoU=0.813 with teacher forcing). The bottleneck has shifted from the VISION side to the LANGUAGE side — Qwen's autoregressive generation during inference produces embeddings that are weaker than the teacher-forced embeddings. The model learned to segment, but not to independently describe what to segment.

next_steps.action: Research the alignment gap between Qwen teacher-forcing and inference
next_steps.rationale: Val mIoU=0.813 proves the segmentation path works. The 0.547 test mIoU on same-distribution data proves the inference path is the bottleneck. Closing this gap is the highest-leverage improvement.
approaches: Scheduled sampling: gradually replace teacher-forced tokens with model-generated tokens during training
Inference-time refinement: use SAM mask outputs to re-rank/filter Qwen descriptions
Contrastive training on Qwen outputs: force inference-time embeddings to match teacher-time embeddings
Separate the description generation from segmentation: use frozen Qwen for descriptions, train only the segmentation path

next_steps.action: Run Phase 1 only (50 epochs) to establish vision engine ceiling
next_steps.rationale: Current config: phase1_epochs=50, num_epochs=50. This isolates the projector+SAM training completely. If val mIoU continues climbing past 0.657 with more epochs, the vision engine has room to grow. If it plateaus, the ceiling is architectural.

next_steps.action: Diversify training data to fix RWTD overfitting
next_steps.rationale: RWTD test mIoU dropped 20% during Phase 2 due to ADE20K distribution bias. Adding texture crop data (RWTD subset, BSDS500, DTD) to training would improve generalization.
25% of test samples predict k=1 (model collapses to single texture). A separate count prediction head trained on image features (not text tokens) could improve inference-time texture count accuracy.
next_steps.approach: Small MLP on SAM backbone features → predicts K, used to mask excess channels during inference

next_steps.rationale: BSDS500 (boundary detection), DTD (texture classification) are unseen by SAM3 and would measure true generalization beyond ADE20K/RWTD.
